theorem_statement:
Young's convolution inequality.

Let \(1 \le p,q,r < \infty\) satisfy
\[
1 + \frac1r = \frac1p + \frac1q.
\]
Let \(f \in L^p(\mathbb{R})\) and \(g \in L^q(\mathbb{R})\), and define their convolution by
\[
(f*g)(x) := \int_{\mathbb{R}} f(x-y)g(y)\,dy.
\]
Then \(f*g \in L^r(\mathbb{R})\) and
\[
\|f*g\|_{L^r} \le \|f\|_{L^p}\,\|g\|_{L^q}.
\]

This is the classical finite-exponent form of Young's convolution inequality on \(\mathbb{R}\).
raw_proof_text:
We prove that if \(1 \le p,q,r < \infty\) satisfy
\[
1 + \frac1r = \frac1p + \frac1q,
\]
and if \(f \in L^p(\mathbb{R})\), \(g \in L^q(\mathbb{R})\), then
\[
\|f*g\|_{L^r} \le \|f\|_{L^p}\,\|g\|_{L^q}.
\]

Throughout the proof we work with absolute values, so it is enough to estimate the convolution of \(|f|\) and \(|g|\). Indeed,
\[
|(f*g)(x)|
\le
\int_{\mathbb{R}} |f(x-y)|\,|g(y)|\,dy.
\]
Thus replacing \(f\) and \(g\) by \(|f|\) and \(|g|\) can only increase the left-hand side. So we may assume from the start that \(f,g \ge 0\).

We split the proof into two cases.

## Case 1: one of the exponents equals 1

Suppose first that \(p=1\). Then the relation
\[
1 + \frac1r = \frac11 + \frac1q
\]
reduces to
\[
\frac1r = \frac1q,
\]
so \(r=q\).

We now estimate \(\|f*g\|_{L^q}\). For each \(x\),
\[
(f*g)(x) = \int_{\mathbb{R}} f(y) g(x-y)\,dy.
\]
Applying Minkowski's integral inequality gives
\[
\|f*g\|_{L^q_x}
=
\left\| \int_{\mathbb{R}} f(y) g(x-y)\,dy \right\|_{L^q_x}
\le
\int_{\mathbb{R}} |f(y)|\, \|g(x-y)\|_{L^q_x}\,dy.
\]
But translation preserves the \(L^q\)-norm, so
\[
\|g(x-y)\|_{L^q_x} = \|g\|_{L^q}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\|f*g\|_{L^q}
\le
\int_{\mathbb{R}} |f(y)|\,\|g\|_{L^q}\,dy
=
\|g\|_{L^q}\int_{\mathbb{R}} |f(y)|\,dy
=
\|f\|_{L^1}\,\|g\|_{L^q}.
\]
Since here \(r=q\), this is exactly
\[
\|f*g\|_{L^r} \le \|f\|_{L^p}\,\|g\|_{L^q}.
\]

The case \(q=1\) is completely symmetric. Then \(r=p\), and the same argument gives
\[
\|f*g\|_{L^p} \le \|f\|_{L^p}\,\|g\|_{L^1}.
\]
So the theorem is proved whenever \(p=1\) or \(q=1\).

From now on we assume
\[
p>1, \qquad q>1.
\]

## Case 2: \(p>1\) and \(q>1\)

We first note that the exponent relation implies
\[
\frac1r = \frac1p + \frac1q - 1.
\]
Since \(1/q \le 1\), we get
\[
\frac1r \le \frac1p,
\]
so \(r \ge p\). Similarly, \(r \ge q\).

Define two auxiliary exponents \(s\) and \(t\) by
\[
\frac1s := \frac1p - \frac1r,
\qquad
\frac1t := \frac1q - \frac1r.
\]
Because \(r \ge p\) and \(r \ge q\), these are nonnegative quantities, so \(s,t \in [1,\infty]\). Also,
\[
\frac1r + \frac1s + \frac1t
=
\frac1r + \left(\frac1p - \frac1r\right) + \left(\frac1q - \frac1r\right)
=
\frac1p + \frac1q - \frac1r
=
1,
\]
where in the last step we used
\[
1 + \frac1r = \frac1p + \frac1q.
\]
Thus the exponents \(r,s,t\) are Hölder-conjugate in the sense needed for a three-factor Hölder inequality.

Now fix \(x \in \mathbb{R}\). Consider the integrand \(f(x-y)g(y)\). We factor it as
\[
f(x-y)g(y)
=
\bigl(f(x-y)^{p/r} g(y)^{q/r}\bigr)
\cdot
f(x-y)^{1-p/r}
\cdot
g(y)^{1-q/r}.
\]
Apply Hölder's inequality in the variable \(y\) with exponents \(r,s,t\). Since
\[
\frac1r + \frac1s + \frac1t = 1,
\]
we obtain
\[
(f*g)(x)
=
\int_{\mathbb{R}} f(x-y)g(y)\,dy
\le
\left(\int_{\mathbb{R}} f(x-y)^p g(y)^q\,dy\right)^{1/r}
\left(\int_{\mathbb{R}} f(x-y)^{(1-p/r)s}\,dy\right)^{1/s}
\left(\int_{\mathbb{R}} g(y)^{(1-q/r)t}\,dy\right)^{1/t}.
\]
We now simplify the exponents in the second and third factors. By the definition of \(s\),
\[
\frac1s = \frac1p - \frac1r = \frac{r-p}{pr},
\]
so
\[
s = \frac{pr}{r-p}.
\]
Hence
\[
(1-p/r)s = \left(\frac{r-p}{r}\right)\frac{pr}{r-p} = p.
\]
Similarly,
\[
(1-q/r)t = q.
\]
Therefore
\[
(f*g)(x)
\le
\left(\int_{\mathbb{R}} f(x-y)^p g(y)^q\,dy\right)^{1/r}
\left(\int_{\mathbb{R}} f(x-y)^p\,dy\right)^{1/s}
\left(\int_{\mathbb{R}} g(y)^q\,dy\right)^{1/t}.
\]
Since translation preserves the \(L^p\)-norm,
\[
\int_{\mathbb{R}} f(x-y)^p\,dy = \int_{\mathbb{R}} f(u)^p\,du = \|f\|_{L^p}^p.
\]
Also,
\[
\int_{\mathbb{R}} g(y)^q\,dy = \|g\|_{L^q}^q.
\]
So
\[
(f*g)(x)
\le
\left(\int_{\mathbb{R}} f(x-y)^p g(y)^q\,dy\right)^{1/r}
\|f\|_{L^p}^{p/s}
\|g\|_{L^q}^{q/t}.
\]
Now
\[
\frac{p}{s} = p\left(\frac1p - \frac1r\right) = 1 - \frac{p}{r},
\qquad
\frac{q}{t} = q\left(\frac1q - \frac1r\right) = 1 - \frac{q}{r}.
\]
Thus
\[
(f*g)(x)
\le
\left(\int_{\mathbb{R}} f(x-y)^p g(y)^q\,dy\right)^{1/r}
\|f\|_{L^p}^{1-p/r}
\|g\|_{L^q}^{1-q/r}.
\]
Raise both sides to the power \(r\):
\[
(f*g)(x)^r
\le
\|f\|_{L^p}^{\,r-p}
\|g\|_{L^q}^{\,r-q}
\int_{\mathbb{R}} f(x-y)^p g(y)^q\,dy.
\]
Integrate in \(x\):
\[
\int_{\mathbb{R}} (f*g)(x)^r\,dx
\le
\|f\|_{L^p}^{\,r-p}
\|g\|_{L^q}^{\,r-q}
\int_{\mathbb{R}}\int_{\mathbb{R}} f(x-y)^p g(y)^q\,dy\,dx.
\]
By Tonelli's theorem, since all functions are nonnegative,
\[
\int_{\mathbb{R}}\int_{\mathbb{R}} f(x-y)^p g(y)^q\,dy\,dx
=
\int_{\mathbb{R}} g(y)^q \left(\int_{\mathbb{R}} f(x-y)^p\,dx\right)dy.
\]
Again using translation invariance,
\[
\int_{\mathbb{R}} f(x-y)^p\,dx = \|f\|_{L^p}^p.
\]
Hence
\[
\int_{\mathbb{R}}\int_{\mathbb{R}} f(x-y)^p g(y)^q\,dy\,dx
=
\int_{\mathbb{R}} g(y)^q \|f\|_{L^p}^p\,dy
=
\|f\|_{L^p}^p \|g\|_{L^q}^q.
\]
Substituting this back, we find
\[
\int_{\mathbb{R}} (f*g)(x)^r\,dx
\le
\|f\|_{L^p}^{\,r-p}
\|g\|_{L^q}^{\,r-q}
\|f\|_{L^p}^p \|g\|_{L^q}^q
=
\|f\|_{L^p}^r \|g\|_{L^q}^r.
\]
Therefore
\[
\|f*g\|_{L^r}^r \le \|f\|_{L^p}^r \|g\|_{L^q}^r.
\]
Taking the \(r\)-th root gives
\[
\|f*g\|_{L^r} \le \|f\|_{L^p}\,\|g\|_{L^q}.
\]
This completes the proof.
